name: Disaster Preparedness
sourceAttribution: Compiled from FEMA Ready.gov, USGS, NOAA/NWS, CDC, and USFS public-domain guidance. NOT a substitute for official local hazard plans — know your zone.
license: CC-BY-4.0 (compilation); source citations carry their own public-domain license

guides.title: Earthquake — Drop, Cover, Hold On
guides.sourceRef: USGS Earthquake Hazards Program + FEMA Ready.gov Earthquake guidance, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
During shaking:

• DROP to your hands and knees so you don't get knocked over.
• Take COVER under a sturdy desk or table. If no shelter, get next to an interior wall, away from windows.
• HOLD ON to your shelter (or your head) until shaking stops.

Do NOT run outside during shaking — falling debris is the largest cause of injury. Do NOT stand in a doorway (modern doorways are not stronger than other framing and you may be hit by a swinging door).

If in bed, stay there and cover your head with a pillow.

If driving, pull over away from buildings, overpasses, and power lines. Stop. Stay inside the vehicle.

After:
• Expect aftershocks (sometimes large).
• Check yourself and others for injury.
• Smell for gas — if you smell it, leave immediately, do not flip switches.
• If a building is damaged, evacuate.
• Tune to local news for tsunami warnings if near coast.

guides.title: Flood — Turn Around, Don't Drown
guides.sourceRef: FEMA Ready.gov Floods + NOAA/NWS Turn Around Don't Drown campaign, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
If an evacuation order is issued for your area, leave immediately. Do not wait to see what happens.

Never drive through standing or moving water:
• 6 inches of moving water can knock an adult down.
• 12 inches can carry away most cars.
• 18–24 inches can sweep away SUVs and trucks.

If trapped in a building, move to the highest floor. Avoid attics that lack roof access — you can be trapped by rising water. Only go on the roof if needed; signal for help.

After the water recedes:
• Assume all flood water is contaminated (sewage, chemicals, debris).
• Do not drink tap water until authorities confirm it's safe.
• Discard food that touched flood water — including canned goods that aren't sealed.
• Watch for downed power lines and snakes.
• Mold is the next risk — dry everything fast.

guides.title: Hurricane — know your zone, leave early
guides.sourceRef: NOAA National Hurricane Center + FEMA Ready.gov Hurricanes, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
Saffir-Simpson categories:
• Cat 1–2: dangerous wind and storm surge
• Cat 3+: major hurricane; devastating damage. Storm surge is the largest killer.

Before the season starts:
• Know your evacuation zone (county/parish maps).
• Have an evacuation plan and go-bag.
• Know multiple routes inland; coastal roads bottleneck.

When warnings issue:
• If ordered to evacuate, leave well before landfall — roads back up fast.
• If sheltering in place: fill the tub with water, charge devices, secure outdoor items, stay away from windows.
• Have a battery or hand-crank radio.
• Stay through the eye — the back side of the storm can be worse.

After:
• Wait for the all-clear before going outside.
• Watch for downed power lines, gas leaks, flood water, snakes, debris.
• Don't drink tap water until authorities clear it.
• Most hurricane deaths happen during cleanup, not the storm — be careful with chainsaws, ladders, and generators.

guides.title: Wildfire — defensible space and evacuation
guides.sourceRef: USFS + Ready.gov + CalFire public guidance on wildfire preparedness, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
Before fire season:
• Clear dry brush, leaves, woodpiles, and lawn-furniture cushions within 30 feet ("defensible space") of structures.
• Keep gutters and roof clear of leaves.
• Keep an N95 mask per person for smoke.
• Identify multiple evacuation routes.

During red-flag conditions:
• Have a go-bag ready at the door.
• Monitor official alerts. Don't rely on smoke or visible flames as your trigger — fast-moving fires arrive in minutes.
• If asked to evacuate, leave immediately. Many wildfire deaths are people who waited.

If trapped:
• Shelter in a large unburned area (paved lot, plowed field, large clearing).
• Lie face down to stay below smoke and heat.
• Cover with non-synthetic material (cotton, wool — synthetics melt).
• Wet a cloth over nose and mouth.

Smoke health: people with heart or lung conditions and children/elderly are at highest risk. Stay indoors with windows closed when AQI is bad. N95 helps; cloth masks don't filter PM2.5.

guides.title: Tornado — basements, interior rooms, no overpasses
guides.sourceRef: NOAA NWS Storm Prediction Center + Ready.gov Tornadoes, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
Warning signs:
• Dark, often greenish sky
• Large hail
• A loud roar like a freight train
• Low-lying cloud rotation
• A visible funnel (often the last sign)

Get to shelter:
• Best: a basement or storm cellar.
• Next best: an interior room on the lowest floor — bathroom, closet, hallway. Away from windows.
• Cover your head and neck. Put a mattress, helmet, or thick blanket over yourself.

Mobile homes are NOT safe in tornadoes. Leave for sturdier shelter even in a watch.

If caught outside with no shelter:
• Lie flat in a ditch or low spot. Cover your head.
• Do NOT shelter under overpasses — they channel and amplify wind.
• Do NOT try to outrun a tornado in a car if traffic is heavy.

Watch = conditions favor tornadoes. Be alert. Warning = tornado spotted or imminent. Take shelter NOW.

guides.title: Tsunami — feel it, see it, hear it, GO
guides.sourceRef: USGS + NOAA Pacific Tsunami Warning Center public guidance, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
Natural warning signs (you may have minutes, not hours):
• Strong or long-duration earthquake near the coast — feel it, leave.
• Sudden ocean recession — water sucked away from the shore — leave immediately.
• A loud roar from the ocean.

If you feel/see/hear any of these on the coast, do NOT wait for an official warning. Go immediately to high ground (at least 100 feet elevation) or as far inland as you can. Run, walk, drive — whatever's fastest.

Official warnings:
• Tsunami Warning: tsunami is expected. Evacuate immediately.
• Tsunami Advisory/Watch: be alert; follow local guidance.

The first wave is rarely the largest. Stay on high ground for hours until official all-clear. Subsequent waves can come 15 minutes to an hour apart and be worse.

Know your community's tsunami evacuation route signs. Practice it. In some places you have less than 10 minutes.

guides.title: Winter storm — shelter and CO safety
guides.sourceRef: NOAA NWS Winter Weather + CDC Carbon Monoxide guidance, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
During a major winter storm, sheltering in place is usually safer than travel.

In your home:
• Keep heat on — let faucets drip slightly to prevent frozen pipes.
• Insulate pipes along outside walls.
• Stay hydrated and fed.

Carbon monoxide is the #1 winter-storm killer indoors. NEVER run any of these inside (or in an attached garage, even with the door open):
• Generators
• Charcoal or gas grills
• Camp stoves
• Vehicle engines

Install battery-backed CO detectors on every level. If anyone feels headachy, dizzy, or nauseated, get outside into fresh air and call emergency services.

If you must travel:
• Keep a vehicle kit: blankets, food, water, shovel, sand or kitty litter (for tire traction), flashlight, phone charger.
• If stranded, stay with the car. Run the engine briefly to warm up but ONLY with a window cracked AND after clearing snow from the tailpipe.
• Tie a bright cloth to the antenna to be visible.

guides.title: Volcanic eruption — ash, lahar, and gases
guides.content:
Three main hazards:

1. Ashfall: fine glass-like particles. Damages lungs, eyes, machinery.
• Stay indoors. Close windows and doors; tape gaps if needed.
• Outside: N95 mask, goggles, long sleeves.
• Do not drive through ash; it ruins engines and reduces visibility to near zero.
• Brush ash off roofs — wet ash is heavy and can collapse roofs.

2. Lahar (volcanic mudflow): fast-moving slurry of ash, water, rock. Can travel many miles down valleys.
• Evacuate to higher ground perpendicular to the valley.
• Bridges may be destroyed.

3. Gases (especially CO2 and SO2): can pool in low areas and suffocate.
• Avoid valleys, low spots, basements near active vents.

Follow USGS Volcano Notification Service alerts. Have an evacuation route that does NOT follow a river valley.

guides.title: Pandemic basics
guides.sourceRef: CDC public guidance on respiratory pandemics, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
A pandemic differs from acute disasters: long duration, distributed risk, sustained behavior change required.

Layered protection (no single layer is perfect):
• Vaccination per current public health guidance for the specific pathogen.
• Hand hygiene: 20 seconds soap and water, or alcohol-based sanitizer.
• Stay home when sick. Isolate per current guidance.
• Mask use during high transmission, especially indoors and in crowded settings. N95/KN95 for higher protection.
• Ventilation indoors: open windows, use HEPA air cleaners.
• Reduce gatherings during peaks.

Supplies to have on hand:
• 2–4 weeks of food and water in case of supply disruption.
• Routine medications: 30–90 day supply.
• Thermometer, OTC fever reducers, hydration solutions.
• Masks for the household.

Follow guidance from CDC and your local public-health authority. Pandemic protocols change as evidence develops; ignore social-media rumors.
